QF.id: XIII Introduction to the Underlying Markets_77

question.text: If an investor holds a bond portfolio worth Rs 10 crore with Modified Duration of 6.2 and wants to hedge against a 1% rise in yields, what is the approximate loss they are trying to protect against?

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Approximate loss = Modified Duration × Δyield × Portfolio value = 6.2 × 0.01 × 10,00,00,000 = Rs 62,00,000 = Rs 62 lakhs. This is the first-order estimate of loss from a 1% parallel yield curve shift. **The Trap:** Rs 6.2 lakhs uses 0.1% instead of 1%. Rs 6.2 crore multiplies incorrectly. Rs 31 lakhs uses 0.5% instead of 1%. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series IV Ch1 covers this calculation. This motivates why IR derivatives (futures, options) are used for hedging — Series IV Ch5 covers the hedging implementation. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** This calculation is the starting point for sizing an IR futures hedge — the number of futures contracts needed = Portfolio DV01 / Futures DV01.
